1: Psalm 102 bears the only full narrative superscription in the Psalter: תְּפִלָּה לְעָנִי כִי-יַעֲטֹף וְלִפְנֵי יְהוָה יִשְׁפֹּךְ שִׂיחוֹ (tĕpillāh lĕʿānî kî-yaʿăṭōp wĕlipnê yĕhwāh yishpōk sîḥô, "A prayer of the afflicted man when he faints and pours out his anguish before the LORD"). ʿĀnaṭ: to be faint/overwhelmed (Psalms 107:5: the hungry and thirsty who "fainted in distress"; Lamentations 2:19). Yishpōk sîḥô: pours out his complaint/meditation (shāpak: to pour out — used of water, blood, and (here) of inner speech; sîaḥ: meditation, complaint, inner discourse — Psalms 55:2,17; Psalms 64:1). שִׁמְעָה יְהוָה תְּפִלָּתִי וְשַׁוְעָתִי אֵלֶיךָ תָבוֹא (shimmĕʿāh yĕhwāh tĕpillātî wĕshaʿwātî ʾêlêkā tābôʾ, "Hear my prayer, O LORD; let my cry come to you!") — the initial petition: both tĕpillāh (the structured prayer) and shaʿwāh (the raw cry of distress — Psalms 18:6; Psalms 22:24).
2: אַל-תַּסְתֵּר פָּנֶיךָ מִמֶּנִּי בְּיוֹם צַר-לִי הַטֵּה-אֵלַי אָזְנֶךָ בְּיוֹם אֶקְרָא מַהֵר עֲנֵנִי (ʾal-tastêr pānêkā mimmenî bĕyôm ṣar-lî haṭṭêh-ʾêlay ʾoznĕkā bĕyôm ʾeqrāʾ mahêr ʿănênî, "Do not hide your face from me when I am in trouble! Lean down and listen; answer me quickly when I call!") — haster pānim: the hiding of the divine face (Psalms 13:1; Psalms 27:9; Psalms 88:14 — the covenant abandonment formula, as in Deuteronomy 31:17-18). HaṭṬêh ʾoznĕkā: incline your ear (as a person bends to catch a faint sound — Psalms 17:6; Psalms 31:2; Psalms 71:2). Mahêr ʿănênî: quickly answer me — the urgency of the dying man who cannot wait for a leisurely divine response.
3: כִּי-כָלוּ בְעָשָׁן יָמָי וְעַצְמוֹתַי כְּמוֹקֵד נִחָרוּ (kî-khālû bĕʿāshān yāmāy wĕʿaṣmôtay kĕmôqêd niḥārû, "For my days are vanishing like smoke, and my bones burn like a blazing hearth!") — kālû bĕʿāshān: consumed in smoke (the days burning away like a fire that produces smoke but no substance — Psalms 37:20; Isaiah 51:6: "the heavens will vanish like smoke"). ʿAṣmôtay kĕmôqêd niḥārû: my bones are scorched like a blazing hearth (môqêd: a burning/fire, a hearth; ḥārar: to scorch, burn up). The simultaneous experience: the life evaporating like smoke above while the bones burn from within — an interior fire consuming the very frame of the person.
4: הוּכָּה כָעֵשֶׂב וַיִּבַשׁ לִבִּי כִּי-שָׁכַחְתִּי מֵאֲכֹל לַחְמִי (hûkkāh kāʿêseb wayyibashshô libbî kî-shākhaktî mêʾăkhōl laḥmî, "My heart is struck down like grass and has dried up; I have even forgotten to eat!") — hûkkāh kāʿêseb: struck down like grass (the image of grass cut down and withering in summer heat — Psalms 37:2; Psalms 90:5-6; Isaiah 40:6-8: "all flesh is grass"). Shākhaktî mêʾăkhōl laḥmî: I have forgotten to eat bread — the most basic physical need forgotten: the level of suffering that strips appetite. This is the body-soul connection: the heart drying up causes the forgetting of food; the physical and interior devastation are one.
5: מִקּוֹל אַנְחָתִי דָּבְקָה עַצְמִי לִבְשָׂרִי (miqqôl ʾanḥātî dābĕqāh ʿaṣmî livsārî, "From the sound of my groaning my bones cling to my skin!") — ʾanḥāh: groaning (the involuntary sound of deep distress — Ezekiel 21:6: "groan before their eyes, groan bitterly"; Lamentations 1:22). Dābĕqāh ʿaṣmî livsārî: my bones cling to my flesh — the wasting away of extreme suffering where there is no more flesh between skin and bone (as in Ezekiel 37:7-8 where the dry bones have no flesh). The progressive physical decay mirrors the inner emptiness of the forsaken.
6: דָּמִיתִי לִקְאַת מִדְבָּר הָיִיתִי כְּכוֹס חֳרָבוֹת (dāmîtî liqĕʾat midbār hāyîtî kĕkhôs ḥărābôt, "I am like a desert owl, like an owl haunting the ruins!") — qāʾat: a pelican or desert bird (Leviticus 11:18; Isaiah 34:11; Zephaniah 2:14 — associated with desolate places). The exact species identification is uncertain (pelican, owl, or desert bird generally) but the habitat is clear: midbār (the wilderness/desert — where no community lives) and ḥărābôt (ruins — where community used to live but was destroyed). The sufferer locates himself not in absence of life but in presence of death and desolation.
7: שָׁקַדְתִּי וָאֶהְיֶה כְּצִפּוֹר בּוֹדֵד עַל-גָּג (shāqadtî wāʾehyeh kĕṣippôr bôdêd ʿal-gāg, "I cannot sleep; I am like a solitary bird on a rooftop!") — shāqad: to wake, to be wakeful (Jeremiah 1:12; Jeremiah 31:28: God "watching over" his word; but here the wakeful wakefulness of the insomniac). Ṣippôr bôdêd ʿal-gāg: a solitary bird on a rooftop — not part of a flock, not in a nest (where birds belong), but alone on an exposed surface, above the community but not part of it. The image conveys both isolation and exposure: the suffering person is visible but unreachable.
8: כָּל-הַיּוֹם חֵרְפוּנִי אוֹיְבָי מְהוֹלְלַי בִּי נִשְׁבָּעוּ (kol-hayyôm ḥêrĕpûnî ʾôyĕbāy mĕhôlĕlay bî nishbāʿû, "My enemies insult me all day long; those who deride me invoke my name as a curse!") — ḥārap: to reproach, taunt, insult (the word used for Goliath's taunting of Israel — 1 Samuel 17:10,25,26). Mĕhôlĕlāy: those who rave against him/those who mock madly (from hālal in its negative sense: raving, boasting wildly against). Bî nishbāʿû: "they swear by me" (using his name as a byword for cursing — as in Isaiah 65:15: "you will leave your name as a curse for my chosen ones"). Social suffering compounding physical suffering.
9: כִּי-אֵפֶר כַּלֶּחֶם אָכָלְתִּי וְשִׁקֻּוַי בִּבְכִי מָסָכְתִּי (kî-ʾêper kalleḥem ʾākāltî wĕshiqqûway bibkhî māsakhtî, "For I eat ashes instead of bread and mix my tears into my drink!") — ʾêper: ashes (the substance of mourning — Job 2:8; Esther 4:1; Isaiah 61:3: "beauty instead of ashes"). Ashes instead of bread: the mourner's refusal of ordinary sustenance, or the desperate impossibility of eating when in profound grief. Bibkhî māsakhtî: I mix with tears (māsak: to mix wine with water — Psalms 75:8; Isaiah 19:14). Tears as the drink mixed into what would otherwise be water — the bitterness of existence.
10: מִפְּנֵי-זַעַמְךָ וְקִצְפֶּךָ כִּי נְשָׂאתַנִי וַתַּשְׁלִיכֵנִי (mippĕnê-zaʿamĕkā wĕqiṣpekā kî nĕśāʾtanî wattashlîkhênî, "because of your fury and wrath; for you have lifted me up only to hurl me down!") — the theological explanation of the suffering: it is the result of the divine wrath (zaʿam: indignation — Psalms 38:3; qeṣep: the fury of the divine anger — Numbers 1:53; Isaiah 60:10). Nĕśāʾtanî wattashlîkhênî: "you lifted me up and you threw me down" — the double divine action: God first elevated the person and then cast them away (as a wave lifts and crashes — or as Jonah was lifted by the sea and thrown to the fish). The suffering is not random but theologically located in the divine reaction.
11: יָמַי כְּצֵל נָטוּי וַאֲנִי כָּעֵשֶׂב אִיבָשׁ (yāmay kĕṣêl nāṭûy waʾănî kāʿêseb ʾîbāsh, "My days are like a fading shadow at evening; I am withering like grass!") — kĕṣêl nāṭûy: like an extended/lengthened shadow (the long shadow of late afternoon that signals the coming end of day — Psalms 109:23: "I am gone like a shadow at evening"). Kāʿêseb ʾîbāsh: withering like grass (Isaiah 40:7: "the grass withers, the flower fades when the breath of the LORD blows on it"). The life is not just ending but the specific mode of ending is evening-shadow (disappearing without remainder) and dry grass (nothing left to burn).
12: וְאַתָּה יְהוָה לְעוֹלָם תֵּשֵׁב וְזִכְרְךָ לְדֹר וָדֹר (wĕʾattāh yĕhwāh lĕʿôlām têshêb wĕzikrĕkā lĕdōr wādōr, "But you, LORD, are enthroned forever; your name endures throughout every generation!") — the psalm's central hinge: the great "But you" (wĕʾattāh) that stands in absolute contrast to everything in vv3-11. The suffering person's days vanish like smoke (v3) and shadow (v11); YHWH sits enthroned forever (lĕʿôlām). The contrast is total: the Psalmist is radically mortal; God is radically permanent. Zikrĕkā lĕdōr wādōr: your name-that-is-remembered generation to generation — the divine name is the covenant identity that outlasts every human generation (Exodus 3:15).
13: אַתָּה תָקוּם תְּרַחֵם צִיּוֹן כִּי-עֵת לְחֶנְנָהּ כִּי-בָא מוֹעֵד (ʾattāh tāqûm tĕraḥêm ṣiyyôn kî-ʿêt lĕḥennāh kî-bāʾ môʿêd, "You will arise and have mercy on Zion, for the time to show her favor has come — the appointed season is here!") — the confidence built on v12's contrast: if YHWH is enthroned forever, then his arising (qûm: the warrior's rising from the throne) to act in compassion is certain. ʿÊt lĕḥennāh: the time of her favor (the appointed moment of divine grace). Môʿêd: the appointed season (the word for the festival calendar — Lev 23; the divine appointment is not vague but timed). The Psalmist shifts from individual suffering to national hope in Zion's restoration.
14: כִּי-רָצוּ עֲבָדֶיךָ אֶת-אֲבָנֶיהָ וְאֶת-עֲפָרָהּ יְחֹנֵנוּ (kî-rāṣû ʿăbādêkā ʾet-ʾăbānêhā wĕʾet-ʿăpārāh yĕḥōnênû, "For your servants hold her very stones dear and take pity even on her dust!") — the reason God will show favor to Zion: his servants (those still faithful) love even the rubble. Rāṣû: to be pleased with, to favor (the covenant acceptance word — Isaiah 40:2: "her iniquity is pardoned, she has received from the LORD's hand double for all her sins"). The servants' love for the ruins is itself the sign of the covenant relationship that makes restoration possible: the stones and dust of a destroyed Zion still bind the hearts of the faithful who expect restoration.
15: וְיִירְאוּ גוֹיִם אֶת-שֵׁם יְהוָה וְכָל-מַלְכֵי הָאָרֶץ אֶת-כְּבוֹדֶךָ (wĕyîrĕʾû gôyim ʾet-shêm yĕhwāh wĕkhol-malkê hāʾāreṣ ʾet-kĕbôdêkā, "The nations will stand in awe of the name of the LORD, and all the kings of the earth will reverence your glory!") — the eschatological scope: Zion's restoration is not merely national recovery but the occasion for universal acknowledgment. Yîrĕʾû gôyim: the nations will fear (Psalms 67:7; Isaiah 60:3: "nations will come to your light, and kings to the brightness of your dawn"). The shêm (name) and kābôd (glory) of Psalms 96:3 appear again — the missionary goal of the enthronement psalms fulfilled through Zion's restoration.
16: כִּי-בָנָה יְהוָה צִיּוֹן נִרְאָה בִּכְבוֹדוֹ (kî-bānāh yĕhwāh ṣiyyôn nirʾāh bikhbôdô, "For the LORD will rebuild Zion and reveal himself in his glory!") — bānāh: to build (used for the divine rebuilding of the ruins of exile — Isaiah 44:26,28; Psalms 45:13; Amos 9:11: "I will restore the fallen booth of David"). Nirʾāh bikhbôdô: he will appear in his glory (the niphal of rāʾāh: to be seen/to appear — the divine self-manifestation). The rebuilding of Zion is the occasion of the divine self-disclosure: the construction project is simultaneously a theophany.
17: פָּנָה אֶל-תְּפִלַּת הָעַרְעָר וְלֹא-בָזָה אֶת-תְּפִלָּתָם (pānāh ʾel-tĕpillat hāʿărʿār wĕlōʾ-bāzāh ʾet-tĕpillātām, "He listens to the prayer of the destitute and does not despise their prayer!") — ʿărʿār: the destitute/isolated one (from ʿārar: to be stripped bare, to be like a blasted tree in the desert — Jeremiah 17:6: "he will be like a shrub in the desert"). Lōʾ-bāzāh: he did not despise (the negative of bāzāh: to hold in contempt — Psalms 22:24: "he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one"). The stripped bare, isolated sufferer's prayer reaches God; the socially destitute are not beneath divine attention.
18: תִּכָּתֶב זֹאת לְדוֹר אַחֲרוֹן וְעַם נִבְרָא יְהַלֶּל-יָהּ (tikkāteb zōʾt lĕdôr ʾaḥărôn wĕʿam nibrāʾ yĕhallel-yāh, "Let this be written for a future generation, so that a people not yet created will praise the LORD!") — tikkāteb: let it be written (the passive imperative — the act of writing this psalm is itself commanded as a future-oriented act). Lĕdôr ʾaḥărôn: for the last/coming generation (not just the next generation but the culminating one — the eschatological community). ʿAm nibrāʾ: a people that will be created/newly born (bārāʾ: the creation verb of Gen 1 applied to a future people who do not yet exist). The Psalmist writes for a community that does not yet exist — the text surviving its author to serve a not-yet-born readership.
19: כִּי-הִשְׁקִיף מִמְּרוֹם קָדְשׁוֹ יְהוָה מִשָּׁמַיִם אֶל-אֶרֶץ הִבִּיט (kî-hishqîp mimmĕrôm qōdshô yĕhwāh mishshāmayim ʾel-ʾāreṣ hibbîṭ, "That the LORD looked down from his holy height; from heaven he gazed upon the earth!") — shāqap (hiphil: to look down from a height — Genesis 26:8; Numbers 21:20; Psalms 85:11). Mimmĕrôm qōdshô: from his holy height (the exalted position from which the universal sovereign surveys his realm — Isaiah 57:15: "thus says the One who is high and lifted up, who inhabits eternity, whose name is Holy"). The downward divine gaze from heaven to earth is the movement of divine compassion (Psalms 14:2; Psalms 33:13-14).
20: לִשְׁמֹעַ אֶנְקַת אָסִיר לְפַתֵּחַ בְּנֵי תְמוּתָה (lishĕmōaʿ ʾenqat ʾāsîr lĕpattêaḥ bĕnê tĕmûtāh, "to hear the groaning of the prisoner and release those condemned to death!") — ʾenqat ʾāsîr: the groaning of the prisoner (the gut-sound of the captive with no power of self-liberation — Exodus 2:24: "God heard their groaning and remembered his covenant"). Bĕnê tĕmûtāh: the sons of death/those appointed to die (from māwet: death; bĕnê: sons = those belonging to the category of). The divine hearing from the heights (v19) aims specifically at the lowest and most helpless: the prisoner and the death-row occupant.
21: לְסַפֵּר בְּצִיּוֹן שֵׁם יְהוָה וּתְהִלָּתוֹ בִּירוּשָׁלִָם (lĕsappêr bĕṣiyyôn shêm yĕhwāh ûtĕhillātô bîrûshālāyim, "so that the name of the LORD would be proclaimed in Zion and his praise sung in Jerusalem!") — the goal of divine rescue: not merely the prisoner's liberty but the proclamation of the divine name in Zion. God's salvation aim is always simultaneously soteriological (saving the captive) and doxological (praising his name). The circle completes: the individual's rescue (vv1-11) leads to Zion's restoration (vv13-16) leads to the universal mission (v15) leads to the declaration of praise in Jerusalem (v21).
22: בְּהִקָּבֵץ עַמִּים יַחְדָּו וּמַמְלָכוֹת לַעֲבֹד אֶת-יְהוָה (bĕhiqqābêṣ ʿammîm yaḥdāw ûmamlākhôt laʿăbōd ʾet-yĕhwāh, "when peoples and kingdoms assemble together to worship the LORD!") — the culminating vision: ʿammîm yaḥdāw (peoples together — the universal gathering); mamlākhôt (kingdoms — the political structures of the world). Laʿăbōd: to serve/worship (the same verb as Psalms 100:2: "serve the LORD with joy"). The individual's prayer (vv1-2) has expanded in successive circles to encompass the entire assembly of the world's peoples and kingdoms worshipping before YHWH in Zion — the eschatological fulfillment of Isaiah 2:2-4; Psalms 60:3; Revelation 21:24-26.
23: עִנָּה בַדֶּרֶךְ כֹּחִי קִצַּר יָמָי (ʿinnāh badderek kōḥî qiṣṣar yāmāy, "He has sapped my strength in midcourse; he has shortened my days!") — the psalm returns to the individual speaker's crisis. ʿInnāh: to afflict, to humiliate (ʿānāh piel: used of afflicting the poor — Exodus 22:21-22; here of God reducing the Psalmist's strength). Badderek: in the road/midway (the interruption of the journey before reaching its destination). Qiṣṣar yāmāy: he cut short my days (qāṣar: to be short/harvest; the metaphor of harvest applied to the lifespan — a life cut short before ripening). The transition back from universal hope to personal lament makes the contrast of vv24-28 more dramatic.
24: אֹמַר אֵלִי אַל-תַּעֲלֵנִי בַּחֲצִי יָמָי (ʾōmar ʾêlî ʾal-taʿălênî baḥăṣî yāmāy, "I said, 'O my God, do not take me away in the middle of my life!'") — baḥăṣî yāmāy: in the half of my days (mid-life — Psalms 55:23: "men of blood and treachery will not live out half their days"; Isaiah 38:10: Hezekiah's prayer "in the middle of my days I must depart"). The Psalmist prays against a premature death — the life cut short (v23) protests its shortness. בְּדוֹר דּוֹרִים שְׁנוֹתֶיךָ (bĕdôr dôrîm shĕnôtêkā, "your years span generation after generation!") — the contrast immediately introduced: God's years span the generations while the Psalmist's days are cut in half. This contrast prepares the great creation-hymn of vv25-27.
25: לְפָנִים הָאָרֶץ יָסַדְתָּ וּמַעֲשֵׂה יָדֶיךָ שָׁמָיִם (lĕpānîm hāʾāreṣ yāsadtā ûmaʿăśêh yādêkā shāmāyim, "Long ago you laid the earth's foundation, and the heavens are the work of your hands!") — the beginning of the creation-hymn (vv25-27) that is quoted in Hebrews 1:10-12 and applied by the Father to the Son: the pre-incarnate Christ who laid the earth's foundations. Lĕpānîm: formerly, long ago (before the current world order — "in the beginning"). Yāsadtā: you founded/established (the word for laying a foundation — Psalms 24:2; Psalms 78:69; Psalms 89:11; Isaiah 48:13). Maʿăśêh yādêkā: the work of your hands — the heavens as a divine artifact (Psalms 8:3; Psalms 19:1). The Psalmist grounds the prayer for life against divine permanence: the Creator of the cosmos is the one the Psalmist appeals to.
26: הֵמָּה יֹאבֵדוּ וְאַתָּה תַעֲמֹד וְכֻלָּם כַּבֶּגֶד יִבְלוּ (hêmmāh yōʾbêdû wĕʾattāh taʿămōd wĕkhullām kabbegĕd yiblû, "They will perish, but you will endure; they will all wear out like old clothes!") — the astonishing claim: the heavens and earth (the most permanent things imaginable) will perish (ʾābad) while God stands (ʿāmad). Kabbegĕd yiblû: they will wear out like a garment (the homely image of a worn-out coat — Isaiah 51:6: "the earth will wear out like a garment"; Isaiah 50:9: "they will all wear out like a garment"). כַּלְּבוּשׁ תַּחֲלִיפֵם וְיַחֲלֹפוּ (kallĕbûsh taḥălîpêm wĕyaḥălōpû, "You will roll them up like a garment and they will be changed!") — ḥālap (hiphil: to change, exchange — as one changes garments). Hebrews 1:12 quotes this phrase of the Son who will fold up the heavens. The cosmos is not permanent — it is a garment God wears and will change.
27: וְאַתָּה-הוּא וּשְׁנוֹתֶיךָ לֹא יִתָּמּוּ (wĕʾattāh-hûʾ ûshĕnôtêkā lōʾ yittāmmû, "but you are ever the same, and your years will never end!") — wĕʾattāh-hûʾ: "but you, he is" — the divine self-identification formula (Deuteronomy 32:39: "I am He"; Isaiah 41:4; Psalms 43:10,13: "I am He" — the eternal self-consistency of the divine being who cannot change). Shĕnôtêkā lōʾ yittāmmû: your years will not be exhausted/completed (from tāmam: to be completed, to come to an end — used of the completion of Israel's suffering in Psalms 77:9, but God's years never reach completion). Hebrews 1:10-12 applies this entire passage (vv25-27) to the Son, establishing his pre-creational existence and eternal consistency: the same divine nature that the Father ascribes to himself the New Testament ascribes to Christ.
28: בְּנֵי-עֲבָדֶיךָ יִשְׁכֹּנוּ וְזַרְעָם לְפָנֶיךָ יִכּוֹן (bĕnê-ʿăbādêkā yishkōnû wĕzarʿām lĕpānêkā yikkôn, "The children of your servants will live in safety; their descendants will be established in your presence!") — the psalm moves from cosmic permanence to covenantal promise. Because God is unchanging (v27), the servants' children have a foundation. Yishkōnû: they will dwell/inhabit (shākan: the tabernacle-word — to take up residence permanently). Lĕpānêkā yikkôn: before your face they will be established (kônen: firmly set up — the same verb for creation's establishment in Psalms 96:10). The individual who prayed against premature death (v24) receives the promise that the next generation will be established in the divine presence. The personal lament becomes covenantal hope: the sufferer's children are the community of v18 ("a people not yet created who will praise the LORD").
